relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Prayer is taught as endurance without losing heart.
An unjust judge lacks reverence and human regard.
A widow keeps demanding justice.
The judge resists until his own inconvenience moves him.
Persistence breaks through where compassion was absent.
Jesus tells them to hear even the unjust judge.
The Divine will make justice for the crying chosen ones.
The question remains whether trust will be found on earth.
Self-righteous comparison becomes the target of parable.
Two people go to the temple to pray.
The Pharisee prays through comparison and superiority.
His practices become proof of self.
The tax collector stands far off, asking mercy.
Humility goes home justified; self-exaltation is reversed.
Infants are brought for touch, and disciples object.
Jesus opens the way for children.
The reign must be received like a child.
A ruler asks about inheriting age-long life.
Jesus returns goodness to the Divine alone.
The commands are named.
The ruler has guarded them since youth.
The remaining lack is wealth released into care for the poor and following.
Great wealth grips him with grief.
Wealth makes entering the reign difficult.
The camel and needle expose the impossibility of wealth as gateway.
The hearers ask who can be saved/healed.
What is impossible with humans remains possible with the Divine.
Peter names what they have left.
Loss for the reign is not forgotten.
The coming age holds age-long life.
The road to Jerusalem fulfills the prophets concerning the Son of Humanity.
Handing-over to the nations is named plainly.
Death and third-day rising are foretold.
The word remains hidden from the disciples.
Near Jericho, a blind beggar sits beside the way.
Hearing the crowd, he asks what is happening.
Jesus the Nazarene is passing by.
He cries to the Son of David for mercy.
Rebuke cannot silence mercy’s cry.
Jesus stands still and calls him near.
The desire is simple: to see again.
Trust saves/heals and sight returns.
Immediately he follows, and praise rises.
